title: Myocardial Protection -- Technique or Treatment -- Cardioplegic vs Noncardioplegic Techniques
content: Ischemic preconditioning This can be divided into local and remote ischemic preconditioning. Local ischemic preconditioning This type is often used with fibrillatory arrest, where the heart is electrically fibrillated before aortic cross-clamping, inducing global ischemia with fibrillation followed by anoxic arrest. Some degree of myocardial protection is provided by unloading the heart through CPB, which reduces myocardial work through fibrillation and provides ischemic preconditioning. The left ventricle is decompressed before placing the aortic cross-clamp to prevent myocardial distension, subendocardial ischemia, and myofibrillar disruption. Mild hypothermia (32-34 °C) increases permissible global ischemia time to approximately 15 minutes and reduces the amplitude of fibrillation. However, temperatures below 30 °C are avoided as it becomes difficult to defibrillate at such low temperatures. Remote ischemic preconditioning This type is a protective strategy designed to mitigate ischemia-reperfusion injury in a target organ, such as the heart, by inducing brief, repeated episodes of ischemia followed by reperfusion in a remote organ or tissue, typically a limb, before the main ischemic event. The underlying mechanism of remote ischemic preconditioning (RIPC) involves releasing protective factors like adenosine, bradykinin, and opioids into the bloodstream during the remote tissue's ischemic episodes. These factors are believed to trigger signaling pathways that protect the heart by reducing cell death, preserving mitochondrial function, and decreasing oxidative stress during subsequent ischemic events. Additionally, neural pathways may play a role, where sensory nerves in the remote tissue detect ischemia and signal the brain, initiating protective responses in the target organ. RIPC is primarily used in cardiac surgery and interventional cardiology to reduce myocardial damage. The procedure typically involves inflating and deflating a blood pressure cuff on a limb for a few minutes, repeated several times before surgery. While some studies have shown that RIPC can reduce myocardial injury and improve outcomes, others have reported no significant benefit, leading to mixed results in the clinical application of RIPC. These varying outcomes may stem from differences in patient populations, surgical techniques, or the specific RIPC protocols used. Despite these inconsistencies, RIPC remains an active area of research, with ongoing studies exploring its potential benefits and underlying mechanisms.

title: Myocardial Protection -- Technique or Treatment -- Cardioplegic vs Noncardioplegic Techniques
content: Although subsequent experimental studies refined cardioplegic techniques, noncardioplegic methods have resurfaced. For example, continuous coronary perfusion is used during aortic root surgery, or surgeons may omit the cross-clamp and induce fibrillatory arrest in cases of porcelain aorta. Intermittent aortic occlusion regained popularity after early studies on ischemic preconditioning. However, recent randomized controlled trials have shown that ischemic preconditioning, especially RIPC, offers no advantages over cardioplegic techniques. [7] [8]

This is called ischemic preconditioning; preconditioned myocytes can tolerate ischemia better than naive myocytes. [11]

title: Stunned Myocardium (Archived) -- Pathophysiology
content: 2. Production of oxygen-derived free radicals and abnormal calcium flux: During the first few minutes of myocardial reperfusion, free radicals are generated that lead to reperfusion injury. [14] The number of free radicals formed decreases as the number of collateral circulations around the ischemic coronaries increases during the ischemic event. Similarly, as the duration of the ischemic event prolongs, the number of free radicals formed also increases. [14] Duration and severity of ischemia, therefore, are major contributory factors to the degree of stunned myocardium. [15] Similarly, calcium levels start to elevate within 1 to 10 minutes of ischemia. Calcium level stays elevated even after reperfusion and precludes normal cardiac contractility. [16] [17] It is also believed that free radicals, along with elevated calcium generated during ischemic events and early reperfusion, impair the proteins of myocardial contractile machinery or sarcoplasmic reticulum, subsequently leading to myocardial stunning. [18]

title: Myocardial Protection -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Myocardial Reperfusion Injury
content: Myocardial reperfusion injury, occurring upon restoring blood flow to the myocardium, presents various challenges. Myocardial stunning refers to reversible mechanical impairment of cardiac muscle function, while lethal reperfusion injury is primarily driven by reactive oxygen species, intracellular calcium overload, and inflammatory processes. Reperfusion arrhythmias, often treatable with interventions like defibrillation, and the no-reflow phenomenon, where perfusion cannot be re-established in a previously ischemic region despite optimal revascularization, also pose significant risks.

content: Histological studies revealed that ischemia/reperfusion (I/R) injury can cause damage comparable to 24 hours of permanent coronary occlusion within just 30 to 60 minutes. title: Myocardial Stunning and Hibernation -- Continuing Education Activity
content: Coronary artery disease can cause myocardial perfusion deficits, impairing contractility and left ventricular function. Myocardial function may be restored through percutaneous coronary intervention or coronary artery bypass grafting. Research continues to identify viable tissue for revascularization. Myocardial stunning involves transient, reversible contractile dysfunction due to acute ischemia. Despite reperfusion, the myocardium remains metabolically intact without irreversible injury, potentially mimicking permanent myocardial damage and leading to unnecessary interventions. In contrast, myocardial hibernation involves chronically depressed contractility due to prolonged ischemia while maintaining viable cells. Both conditions are more common in patients with coronary artery disease, those undergoing cardiac surgery, or those experiencing acute ischemia from myocardial infarction or shock.

title: Myocardial Stunning and Hibernation -- Pathophysiology
content: The key difference between stunning and hibernation lies in coronary flow. In stunning, resting coronary flow remains unaffected, whereas in hibernation, it decreases. Hibernation is characterized by postischemic dysfunction, which may be acute, subacute, or chronic, following one or more ischemic episodes. The outcome of myocardial hibernation depends on timely hemodynamic improvements, either through prompt revascularization or by reducing myocardial oxygen consumption before cellular death occurs. [14] Several defense mechanisms, known as autoregulatory phenomena, are observed in response to reduced myocardial flow in the hibernating myocardium. These mechanisms include: When a sudden reduction in coronary flow occurs, the energy demands of the hypoperfused myocardium exceed the available supply, creating an imbalance that leads to a reduction in myocardial contractility and energy consumption. This results in a state of "perfusion-contraction matching." [15] [16]

title: Stunned Myocardium (Archived) -- Introduction
content: The myocardium is composed of cardiac muscle fibers. The contraction of these myocardial fibers leads to the contraction of the heart. Prolonged severe reduction of coronary blood flow can seize myocardial contractility in an irreversible fashion as a result of myocardial necrosis. [1] However, the myocardium can be salvaged with early coronary reperfusion. Nonetheless, the viable reperfused myocardium might not return to contracting properly immediately following reperfusion. The subsequent prolonged but transient postischemic dysfunction after reperfusion is known as the stunned myocardium. [2] Similarly, neurologic pathologies can also lead to stunning of the myocardium, and the phenomenon is collectively called neurogenic stunned myocardium. [3]

title: Stunned Myocardium (Archived) -- Epidemiology
content: The exact incidence and prevalence of stunned myocardium are not appreciated as it is a transient process, and routine diagnostics for evaluation of stunned myocardium are not recommended. However, newer studies reveal that patients from all age groups, including the pediatric population on hemodialysis (HD), can develop myocardial stunning. [8] [9] According to some studies, there is a 5 to 26 times increased risk of developing stunned myocardium in patients receiving standard HD sessions with the removal of 1 L to 2 L of fluids. [10] [9] Similarly, a Polish study with a cohort of 120 patients with acute coronary syndrome for the first time revealed one in every four patients had neurogenic stunned myocardium, as evidenced by regional ventricular wall dysfunction remote to the ischemic territory. [11] Future studies are warranted to evaluate the occurrence of stunned myocardium in various populations.

title: Myocardial Protection -- Technique or Treatment -- Continuous vs Intermittent Cardioplegia
content: As cardioplegic techniques advanced, increasingly complex surgeries became feasible, leading to longer cross-clamp times. However, extended cross-clamp times, particularly those exceeding 120 minutes, revealed some limitations in traditional cardioplegia methods. This prompted the scientific community to revisit earlier techniques, such as continuous coronary perfusion, which had fallen out of favor after the Melrose technique failed. Unlike methods that protect the heart by reducing its metabolic demands through electromechanical arrest and hypothermia, continuous coronary perfusion protects the heart by maintaining a steady supply of a normothermic cardioplegic solution. This approach, often referred to as "warm heart surgery" or "aerobic heart surgery," focuses on "increasing supply" rather than "decreasing demand."

title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 926
content: Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures encompass a range of catheter‑based interventions designed to treat coronary artery disease, most notably percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty (ptca) and coronary thrombolysis. Both ptca and coronary thrombolysis are classified as forms of interventional cardiac catheterization, indicating that they are performed through vascular access to the coronary circulation. Ptca is a mechanical technique used to dilate narrowed coronary segments and is a specific subcategory within the broader Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures. Coronary thrombolysis, which employs pharmacologic agents to dissolve thrombotic occlusions, is also a component of Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures and serves the purpose of removal of coronary artery obstruction. A recognized clinical sequela of ptca is the occurrence of recurrent angina after percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty, underscoring the importance of post‑procedure monitoring and possible additional therapeutic measures. Together, these interventional cardiac catheterization procedures provide complementary strategies for restoring coronary patency and optimizing patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 1103
content: Acute myocardial infarction encompasses a spectrum of disorders that all involve injury to the myocardium. Specific forms include Acute Q‑wave infarction – widespread, Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI), and Acute STEMI due to occlusion of distinct coronary branches such as the left coronary artery, the left anterior descending (LAD) artery, the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the diagonal branch of the LAD, the intermediate artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, and the septal branch of the LAD. Additional variants are Acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to circumflex branch occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction occurring during a procedure, and Acute widespread myocardial infarction. Despite the differing anatomic sites of arterial blockage and electrocardiographic patterns (Q‑wave versus ST‑segment elevation), each of these entities shares the common finding site of the myocardium, underscoring that the primary pathological substrate in all these conditions is myocardial tissue damage. Recognizing the specific coronary artery involved helps guide reperfusion strategies and risk stratification, while the universal involvement of the myocardium highlights the need for prompt diagnosis and treatment to preserve cardiac function.

Acute non‑Q wave infarction, acute non‑Q wave infarction – widespread, acute non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder), acute widespread myocardial infarction, postoperative acute myocardial infarction, and subsequent anterior non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction are additional variants of acute myocardial infarction, each reflecting different electrocardiographic patterns, timing, or clinical contexts, yet each also localizes its injury to the myocardial tissue. title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2016
content: Coronary artery disease (تصلب الشريان التاجي) is clinically associated with a range of cardiac conditions and diagnostic procedures. It is linked to cardiac disorders (اضطراب قلبي), congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني), and heart murmurs (نفخة قلبية), reflecting its broad impact on cardiac function and symptomatology. In addition, coronary artery disease is closely connected to the use of cardiac catheterization (قسطرة قلبية) as a key investigative and therapeutic tool. Cardiac disorders and congestive heart failure are also individually associated with cardiac catheterization, underscoring the procedure’s central role in evaluating and managing these interrelated cardiovascular problems. Together, these associations highlight the intertwined nature of coronary artery disease, various cardiac pathologies, and the pivotal role of cardiac catheterization in patient assessment and care.

title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 760
content: Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures encompass several interventional techniques used to restore patency of coronary vessels. Percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty (ptca) is classified as a specific type of both Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures and interventional cardiac catheterization, reflecting its role as a catheter‑based method to dilate stenotic coronary arteries. Coronary thrombolysis is likewise a component of Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures and is considered a form of interventional cardiac catheterization; it serves as the parent concept for the broader procedure described as removal of coronary artery obstruction. The removal of coronary artery obstruction, which is linked to the Arabic term “رأف الوعاء,” is therefore understood as a specific application of coronary thrombolysis aimed at clearing occlusive material from the coronary lumen. Ptca is also associated with the term “رأف الوعاء” through a reference relationship, indicating that both ptca and removal of obstruction share the underlying goal of vessel reperfusion. Clinically, recurrent angina after percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty can occur, highlighting the need for careful follow‑up and possible adjunctive therapies such as coronary thrombolysis or repeat obstruction removal. Together, these interrelated concepts illustrate the hierarchy and functional connections among catheter‑based coronary interventions, their therapeutic categories, and the potential post‑procedural complications that guide patient management.

title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 599
content: Cardioplegic solutions (kardioplegické roztoky) are a specialized subset of medicinal solutions (lékové roztoky) that are used to intentionally induce cardiac arrest (شلّ القلب) during cardiac surgery, providing myocardial protection while the heart is stopped. Extracorporeal circulation auxiliary to open‑heart procedures, which includes the use of a heart‑lung machine, is closely linked to the management of cardiac arrest because it supplies oxygenated blood to the body while the heart is arrested by cardioplegic solutions. This extracorporeal support also underlies a range of other intrathoracic procedures performed with cardiopulmonary bypass. Procedures performed on the aortic valve with cardiopulmonary bypass are categorized as “other intrathoracic procedures on the aortic valve with cardiopulmonary bypass” and are mapped to the broader group of “other operations on valves of the heart.” Similarly, procedures on the coronary arteries performed with cardiopulmonary bypass are described as “other intrathoracic procedures on arteries of the heart with cardiopulmonary bypass” and are mapped to “other operations on vessels of the heart.” These vessel‑focused operations have a direct relationship with the coronary vessels (koronární cévy), emphasizing their relevance to coronary artery disease and revascularization strategies. Together, cardioplegic solutions, extracorporeal circulation, and the various intrathoracic procedures with cardiopulmonary bypass form an integrated framework for safely arresting the heart, maintaining systemic perfusion, and addressing both valvular and coronary pathologies during complex open‑heart surgery. Effective coordination of these elements is essential for optimal myocardial protection, accurate surgical intervention, and improved patient outcomes.
